items.reason: This 1825 act imposes government tolls on trade, distorting incentives, raising costs for merchants and consumers, reducing supply and economic activity in Bristol—a major port—and creating barriers to entry that favor incumbents while stifling competition and prosperity. Such feudal-era revenue tools are obsolete amid modern taxation systems, with unseen costs including foregone trade, innovation, and wealth creation that private markets would foster unhindered.

items.title: Alliance Marine Assurance Company Act 1825
items.summary: A 1825 UK Local Act (c. ccii) incorporating or regulating the Alliance Marine Assurance Company, enabling its marine insurance operations; content available only as PDF, indicating obsolescence.
items.reason: This 200-year-old private act granted special privileges to a specific, long-defunct company, exemplifying government favoritism that distorts markets and equality under law. Superseded by modern general corporate and financial regulations (e.g., Companies Act 2006, FSMA 2000), it serves no purpose today. Keeping it clutters the legal code, raises research costs for businesses/lawyers, and risks unintended confusion in historical claims or interpretations, hindering clarity and prosperity without achieving any ongoing benefits.

items.title: St. James, Poole, Church Act 1825
items.summary: St. James, Poole, Church Act 1825: A local private act concerning the church of St. James in Poole, Dorset, likely authorizing its construction, maintenance, governance, or related local matters.
items.reason: This 199-year-old local act is obsolete, superseded by modern charity law, planning regulations, and property rights. Keeping it imposes unseen costs: clutters the statute book, risks archaic provisions being cited in disputes (raising legal expenses and uncertainty), distorts incentives by preserving outdated institutional relics that hinder clear private property management of church assets through voluntary associations, and exemplifies regulatory accumulation without benefits—deletion promotes institutional clarity and liberty without harming outcomes, as private initiative sustains such institutions today.
